essay_text:
I wake up to the smell of fresh banitsa and the sun on my pillow. As the scent wafts through the apartment, my
sisters and I run to the kitchen to find our grandma with flour smudged across her face. Waiting for the banitsa to
bake is an impossible task as its warm, mouth-watering aroma fills the home until a crispy golden perfection finally
emerges from the oven.
My grandparents live in the small town of Karlovo, nestled beneath the Balkan Mountains. From the balcony of
their tiny top floor apartment, one can see the whole valley lying below and the deep green mountains rising
above. Every summer when visiting my grandparents, I am welcomed by a small crowd of neighbours, who wave
and invite us over for homemade banitsa, the same rich feta-filled traditional Bulgarian pie that woke me up in my
grandparents’ home. This is a treasured escape from bustling London.
Being a child of first-generation immigrants in the UK, I have struggled with my Bulgarian identity. I can remember
my 10-year-old self, bringing my packed lunch to school, like I did every day, and a girl smirking at my tasty but
not-so-appealing messy moussaka while munching on her perfectly cut triangle ham and cheese sandwich. I also
remember a school friend telling me she had a “lie-in” on Sunday and then burst out laughing when I asked what it
was. My English was weaker than that of British children as, although I was growing up bilingual, my first language
was Bulgarian. I felt like an outsider and although I loved Bulgaria, I was embarrassed to share my family’s origin,
fearing judgement.
As my dad’s 50th birthday approached, I decided to surprise him and learn a Bulgarian folk dance: the “Horo”, a
line dance where people hold hands and jump up and down to vigorous orchestral music. It is a challenging
dance, with additive rhythms and up to 520 beats per minute, but knowing that it would make my dad happy drove
me to keep going, just to make him proud and to see the smile on his face. At the party, amongst extended family
and friends, we gorged on traditional Bulgarian food and danced late into the night, hands together – all of us,
people of different ages, backgrounds, nationalities and abilities. I was moved, proud and exhilarated to belong to
this culture and to share the love and the joy of it. At that moment, filled with a sense of freedom and acceptance
of my full identity, I embraced my dual British and Bulgarian heritage.
As time passed, I met more classmates at school and realised that many of us came from all across the world. I
found it fascinating to hear about their customs and they seemed to be genuinely interested to hear about mine,
especially when food was involved. We began to share lunches and I wowed them with my homemade banitsa. I
now invite friends to dinner parties and showcase all I learned from my grandma’s cooking and hospitality. I
realised that our differences make the world a richer and better place as we are all shaped by our heritage and
experiences, and bring forth distinct perspectives. London is a cultural kaleidoscope and I look for and grasp every
opportunity to learn from others’ traditions, be it through a conversation at school, a shared delicacy, or the
exchange of architectural ideals through the Architectural Youth Panel. It is even within these niches that bridges
can be built.
CA Ivanova, Nikki CEEB: 724545 Fall 2021
8 FYED CAID: 25771556
As an international student, I hope to grow the diversity of my university by sharing my rich Bulgarian / British /
European heritage. I look forward to meeting many more individuals from across the world and introducing this
community to the family customs and traditions that made London my Bulgarian home. Let’s see if any of them
can beat my banitsa with their home cuisine!
prompt: Some students have a background, identity, interest, or talent that is so meaningful they believe their application would be incomplete without it. If this sounds like you, then please share your story.
persona_prompt: You are a writer who artfully blends vivid sensory descriptions, particularly those related to food and place, with a reflective exploration of personal identity. You move from specific, heartfelt anecdotes that highlight vulnerability to broader insights about cultural pride and the value of diversity. Your narrative structure is chronological, illustrating a journey of self-acceptance and a confident embrace of a multicultural heritage.
sentence_style: Varied, often beginning with immediate sensory details or simple statements, then expanding into more descriptive clauses. Sentences can be long and flowing to create immersive scenes, balanced by direct, declarative sentences when expressing personal feelings or factual observations. The rhythm is engaging and accessible.
vocabulary_level: Moderate — with a natural and precise use of descriptive adjectives (e.g., 'mouth-watering aroma,' 'vigorous orchestral,' 'cultural kaleidoscope') without being overly complex or academic.
structural_patterns: A clear chronological narrative arc detailing a journey of personal growth and identity formation. Utilizes specific, evocative anecdotes (e.g., banitsa, moussaka, Horo dance) to illustrate internal conflicts and their eventual resolution. Employs contrast (e.g., London vs. Karlovo, embarrassment vs. pride) to highlight transformation, moving from individual experience to broader societal reflections.
distinctive_moves: Opening paragraphs with rich, vivid sensory details that ground the reader immediately in a specific moment or place.
Using food as a powerful central motif to represent cultural identity, familial connection, and a means of sharing heritage.
Skillfully juxtaposing moments of personal vulnerability and struggle with instances of profound cultural pride and self-acceptance.
Employing specific, memorable anecdotes to not only illustrate but also evoke complex emotions and thematic shifts.
Transitioning from an internal, personal struggle to a confident, outward-looking appreciation for global diversity and cultural exchange.
themes_and_preoccupations: The intricate process of forming and embracing a dual cultural identity (immigrant experience).
The profound role of food, family traditions, and shared experiences in establishing cultural belonging.
The journey from feeling like an outsider to achieving a sense of acceptance and pride in one's heritage.
Celebrating and valuing cultural diversity as a means to enrich one's own life and the wider community.
self_presentation: Initially presents as vulnerable and introspective, openly sharing struggles with identity and feelings of being an outsider. Evolves into a confident, proud, and forward-looking individual who has not only embraced their heritage but is also eager to share and celebrate it, positioning themselves as a cultural bridge-builder.
cultural_or_contextual_markers: Deeply rooted in the first-generation immigrant experience in the UK, with strong ties to Bulgarian heritage (specific foods, dances, geographical settings). Explicitly acknowledges and thrives within the multicultural environment of London.
avoid_in_imitation: Employing overly academic, detached, or clinical language that lacks personal warmth.
Relying on abstract generalizations without anchoring them in concrete personal anecdotes or sensory details.
Maintaining a consistently pessimistic, cynical, or purely analytical tone that foregoes emotional resonance or a sense of growth.
sample_sentence: The distinct aroma of my grandmother's simmering borscht, a fragrant blend of beets and dill, always conjures memories of cold Ukrainian winters and stories told in hushed tones around a crackling stove, connecting me to a heritage I once hid.
